threat.title: Session Fixation Attacks in Express.js and Spring Boot Applications
threat.description: Session fixation occurs when an attacker sets or predicts a user's session identifier before the user authenticates, then hijacks the session after authentication. Unlike session hijacking where attackers steal existing valid sessions, fixation attacks force victims to use attacker-controlled session IDs. This vulnerability is particularly prevalent in web applications that fail to regenerate session IDs upon authentication, allowing attackers to bypass authentication entirely and impersonate legitimate users.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker visits target application and obtains a valid session ID (e.g., JSESSIONID=abc123 or connect.sid=xyz789)
2. Attacker crafts a malicious link or form containing the session ID: https://victim-app.com/login?sessionid=abc123
3. Victim clicks the malicious link or is redirected via XSS, accepting the attacker's session ID
4. Victim successfully authenticates using their legitimate credentials while using the attacker's session ID
5. Application fails to regenerate session ID after authentication, maintaining the same session ID
6. Attacker uses the same session ID (abc123) to access the application as the authenticated victim, bypassing all authentication controls and accessing sensitive data, executing transactions, or modifying account settings
prerequisites: Application accepts session IDs from URL parameters, cookies, or hidden form fields
Application does NOT regenerate session ID upon successful authentication
Session ID remains valid after authentication completes
Attacker can deliver session ID to victim (via phishing, XSS, or MITM)
No additional session validation (IP address binding, user-agent checking)

cve_references.description: Session fixation vulnerability in express-openid-connect (versions 2.3.0 - 2.5.1) - fails to regenerate session ID upon authentication
Spring Security WebFlux authorization bypass using wildcard patterns - allows session security bypass
Authorization bypass in Spring Security RegexRequestMatcher - enables session security bypass

real_world_breaches.incident: Okta Support System Breach (Session Hijacking via HAR Files)
real_world_breaches.impact: 134 customers affected, session tokens from HAR files used to hijack 5 customer sessions including 1Password, BeyondTrust, and Cloudflare
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Stolen session tokens from customer support HAR files used to bypass MFA and hijack authenticated sessions

real_world_breaches.impact: Users exposed to session hijacking due to short, persistent session tokens without expiration or RBAC
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Session tokens susceptible to brute-force attacks, never expire, and lack role-based access control

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Session fixation vulnerability allowing account takeover via session ID manipulation

security_research.title: Reliable Protection Against Session Fixation Attacks
security_research.key_finding: Session fixation can only be reliably prevented by regenerating session identifiers upon authentication and privilege escalation

security_research.title: OWASP Session Fixation Attack
security_research.key_finding: Session fixation is one of the most common session management vulnerabilities, affecting applications that accept session IDs from URL parameters or fail to regenerate session IDs after authentication

mitigations.title: Regenerate Session ID Upon Authentication (Express.js & Spring Boot)
mitigations.description: Always regenerate session identifiers immediately after successful authentication and any privilege level changes. This is the primary defense against session fixation attacks and MUST be implemented in all web applications handling authentication.
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified, revoked, and audited
PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Test that session ID changes after successful login
Verify old session ID becomes invalid after regeneration
Confirm session attributes are properly migrated
Test that failed login attempts do not regenerate session
Verify session regeneration occurs on privilege escalation

mitigations.title: Reject Session IDs from URL Parameters
mitigations.description: Configure applications to only accept session identifiers from HTTP-only cookies, never from URL parameters, hidden form fields, or other client-controllable sources.
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
PR.IP-2 - A System Development Life Cycle is implemented
validation_steps: Test that URLs containing sessionid parameters are rejected with 400 error
Verify session is only established via secure HTTP-only cookies
Confirm URL rewriting is disabled in servlet configuration
Test that application functions properly without URL-based session tracking

mitigations.title: Implement Session Validation (IP and User-Agent Binding)
mitigations.description: Bind session identifiers to additional client properties like IP address ranges and User-Agent strings for defense-in-depth.
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
DE.CM-1 - Network monitored to detect anomalous activity
validation_steps: Test that session is invalidated when accessing from different IP
Verify User-Agent changes trigger security alerts
Confirm audit logs capture session validation failures
Test with mobile users switching networks

indicators: Multiple users accessing the same session ID from different IP addresses
Session ID does not change after successful authentication
Session IDs appearing in URL parameters or HTTP GET requests
Same session ID used before and after login events
Rapid session ID enumeration attempts from single IP
Session accessed from geographically distant locations within short timeframe
Session fingerprint mismatches (IP or User-Agent changes)
Authentication success followed by immediate access from different IP

log_queries.query:
let SessionEvents = 
    AppServiceHTTPLogs
    | extend SessionId = extract(@"sessionid=([^;\\s&]+)", 1, CsCookie)
    | where isnotempty(SessionId)
    | extend IpAddress = CIp, Timestamp = TimeGenerated;
SessionEvents
| summarize 
    DistinctIPs = dcount(IpAddress),
    IPs = make_set(IpAddress),
    LoginEvents = countif(CsUriStem has "/login"),
    RequestCount = count(),
    FirstSeen = min(Timestamp),
    LastSeen = max(Timestamp)
    by SessionId
| where DistinctIPs > 1 and LoginEvents > 0
| extend SuspicionScore = DistinctIPs * 10 + LoginEvents * 5
| where SuspicionScore > 15
| order by SuspicionScore desc

index=web_logs sourcetype=access_combined
| rex field=cookie "sessionid=(?<session_id>[^;\\s&]+)"
| where isnotnull(session_id)
| stats 
    dc(clientip) as distinct_ips,
    values(clientip) as ip_addresses,
    count(match(uri_path, "login")) as login_count,
    count as total_requests,
    earliest(_time) as first_seen,
    latest(_time) as last_seen
    by session_id
| where distinct_ips > 1 AND login_count > 0
| table session_id, distinct_ips, ip_addresses, login_count, total_requests
| sort -distinct_ips
http.request.method: "GET" AND (url.query: *sessionid=* OR url.query: *JSESSIONID=*) AND event.outcome: "success"

tools.name: OWASP ZAP
tools.usage: Use Session Fixation scanner in Active Scan to detect vulnerable applications

tools.name: Burp Suite
tools.usage: Test session management by obtaining session ID before auth and verifying if it changes after login
